input_text: Diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disease characterized by elevated blood glucose levels and inadequate insulin release relative to the body's needs.
Between 11 and 14% of adults worldwide have diabetes mellitus.
Type 1 diabetes comprises 5 to 10% of all diagnosed diabetes mellitus cases.

input_text: Other types of diabetes mellitus account for the remainder of diabetes mellitus cases.
The International Diabetes Federation provides information about diabetes mellitus in the publication "About diabetes: Facts and Figures".
The World Health Organization provides key facts about diabetes mellitus in the publication "Diabetes: Key Facts".

The American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee publishes standards of care in diabetes mellitus, including diagnosis and classification, in the publication "Diagnosis and Classification of Diabetes: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2025".
Type 1 diabetes is primarily characterized by inadequate insulin production due to autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells.
Type 2 diabetes is primarily characterized by insulin resistance and a relative insufficiency of insulin.

input_text: Glucose control often proves difficult in hospitalized patients with pre-existing diabetes when other diseases are more acute in these patients.
Restricted physical activity and acute illness worsen hyperglycemia in some hospitalized patients with pre-existing diabetes.

input_text: In patients with type 1 diabetes, one-half of the usual morning dose of intermediate-acting insulin can be given the night or morning before surgery.

In patients with type 1 diabetes, 75 to 80% of the dose of long-acting insulin, such as glargine or detemir, can be given the night or morning before surgery at the usual time of long-acting insulin administration.
During and after surgery, plasma glucose should be measured at least every 2 to 4 hours in patients undergoing surgery.

input_text: Viruses, such as coxsackievirus and severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2, may cause beta-cell destruction indirectly by exposing autoantigens, such as glutamic acid decarboxylase, insulin, proinsulin, insulinoma-associated protein, and zinc transporter ZnT8.
Viruses, such as coxsackievirus and severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2, may cause beta-cell destruction indirectly by activating autoreactive lymphocytes.

Viruses, such as coxsackievirus and severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2, may cause beta-cell destruction indirectly by mimicking molecular sequences of autoantigens, such as glutamic acid decarboxylase, insulin, proinsulin, insulinoma-associated protein, and zinc transporter ZnT8, which stimulate an immune response, also known as molecular mimicry.

input_text: Patients with sensory loss or circulatory impairment should change their socks or stockings daily and not wear constricting clothing, such as garters, socks, or stockings with tight elastic tops.
Shoes for patients with sensory loss or circulatory impairment should fit well, be wide-toed without open heels or toes, and be changed frequently.

Special shoes should be prescribed to patients with sensory loss or circulatory impairment to reduce trauma if the foot is deformed, such as in the case of previous toe amputation, hammer toe, or bunion.
Patients with sensory loss or circulatory impairment should avoid walking barefoot.

input_text: The choice among glucose meter devices is usually based on patient preferences for features such as time to results, which usually ranges from 5 to 30 seconds.
The choice among glucose meter devices is also based on patient preferences for size of display panel, with large screens benefiting patients with poor eyesight.
The choice among glucose meter devices is also based on patient preferences for voice readout, which benefits patients with visual impairment.
The choice among glucose meter devices is also based on patient preferences for smartphone app connectivity.
The choice among glucose meter devices is also based on availability, affordability, and insurance coverage of test strips for patients.
Continuous glucose monitoring systems estimate capillary blood glucose from interstitial glucose detected by a subcutaneous sensor.

Continuous glucose monitoring systems can either provide glucose measurements continuously, known as real-time continuous glucose monitoring, or intermittently when scanned with a device, known as intermittently scanned continuous glucose monitoring.

input_text: Neuropathy is a heterogeneous long-term complication of type 1 diabetes, with multifactorial pathogenesis that includes toxic effects of hyperglycemia and advanced glycation end-products on nerves, and ischemia to nerves from microvascular disease of type 1 diabetes.

Macrovascular complications of type 1 diabetes include atherosclerosis of large vessels leading to angina pectoris and myocardial infarction, transient ischemic attacks and strokes, and peripheral artery disease.
Immune dysfunction is another major long-term complication of type 1 diabetes.
